1:
"And there was a famine in the land, beside the first famine that was in the days of Abraham. And Isaac went unto Abimelech king of the Philistines unto Gerar." The witnesses note the famine driving Isaac to Gerar. On "there was a famine in the land, beside the first famine that was in the days of Abraham," the grammatical voices note the trial. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "there was a famine in the land, beside the first famine that was in the days of Abraham" — a second famine (distinct from the one in Abraham's day, 12:10) came on the land; the trial of Isaac, as of his father; the providence that drove the patriarch from his dwelling; the testing of faith in the land of promise.

On "Isaac went unto Abimelech king of the Philistines unto Gerar," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the journey. Henry and Calvin note that Isaac went to Gerar (the Philistine country, where Abraham had sojourned, ch. 20), to Abimelech (the title of the Philistine kings, perhaps a successor of Abraham's Abimelech); the patriarch seeking refuge from the famine; the journey toward (but not into) Egypt; the setting for God's word (vv.2-5).

On the famine, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the providence: the famine, like Abraham's trials, tested Isaac's faith; the patriarch tempted (as his father) to go down to Egypt for relief, but stayed by God's command (v.2); the trial that occasioned the renewal of the covenant; the testing of the son of promise in the land. The verse displays the famine driving Isaac to Gerar.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the famine — "there was a famine in the land, beside the first famine... in the days of Abraham," a second famine, the trial of Isaac (12:10, Keil, Gill); "Isaac went unto Abimelech... unto Gerar," the journey to the Philistine country (ch. 20, Henry, Calvin); the testing of faith (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the journeying Isaac. The verse marks the famine: a second. The verse displays the Gerar: the refuge. The verse displays the Abimelech: the king. The verse displays the trial: of faith. The famine displays Isaac driven to Gerar. The verse displays the Isaac: journeying. The verse displays the famine: testing. The verse displays the Gerar: sought. The famine driving Isaac to Gerar displays the trial of the son of promise: a famine, beside the first in Abraham's day (12:10), drove Isaac to Abimelech at Gerar — the patriarch tested as his father, seeking refuge, the setting for God's word (vv.2-5). The verse displays the journeying Isaac, met by the LORD (v.2).
2:
"And the LORD appeared unto him, and said, Go not down into Egypt; dwell in the land which I shall tell thee of:" The witnesses note the LORD forbidding Isaac to go to Egypt. On "the LORD appeared unto him," the grammatical voices note the revelation. Ellicott and Gill note that "the LORD appeared unto him, and said, Go not down into Egypt" — God appeared to Isaac (as to Abraham) with direction in the famine; the covenant-God guiding the son of promise; the appearing that gave the command and the covenant-renewal (vv.2-5); the providence over Isaac.

On "Go not down into Egypt," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the command. Calvin and Gill note that God forbade Isaac to go down to Egypt — unlike Abraham (who went to Egypt in the first famine, 12:10), Isaac was to stay in the land of promise; the command keeping the son of promise in Canaan (the land given to the seed); the trial of faith — to stay in the famine-struck land, trusting God, rather than seek relief in Egypt.

On "dwell in the land which I shall tell thee of," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the direction: "dwell in the land which I shall tell thee of" — Isaac was to dwell where God directed (Gerar, the land of promise); the obedience of faith, staying in the land at God's word; the patriarch kept in Canaan; the providence ordering his sojourn. The verse displays the LORD forbidding Isaac to go to Egypt.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the revelation — "the LORD appeared unto him," God guiding Isaac (Ellicott, Gill); "Go not down into Egypt," the command to stay in the land (12:10, Calvin, Gill); "dwell in the land which I shall tell thee of," the obedience of faith (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the directed Isaac. The verse marks the appearing: the LORD. The verse displays the command: not to Egypt. The verse displays the land: of promise. The verse displays the directed Isaac, given the covenant (vv.3-5).
3:
"Sojourn in this land, and I will be with thee, and will bless thee; for unto thee, and unto thy seed, I will give all these countries, and I will perform the oath which I sware unto Abraham thy father;" The witnesses note the renewal of the covenant-promise to Isaac. On "Sojourn in this land, and I will be with thee, and will bless thee," the grammatical voices note the promise. Gill and Clarke note that "Sojourn in this land, and I will be with thee, and will bless thee" — God bade Isaac sojourn in the land (Gerar/Canaan) with the promise of his presence and blessing; the assurance, "I will be with thee" (the great covenant-promise, 28:15; Isaiah 41:10); the blessing on the obedient son of promise.

On "unto thee, and unto thy seed, I will give all these countries," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the land. Calvin and Gill note the promise of the land — "unto thee, and unto thy seed, I will give all these countries"; the renewal to Isaac of the gift of Canaan (to him and his seed, 13:15; 15:18); the covenant-inheritance confirmed to the son.

On "I will perform the oath which I sware unto Abraham thy father," the grammatical-historical note the oath: "I will perform the oath which I sware unto Abraham thy father" — God would keep to Isaac the oath sworn to Abraham (22:16-18); the covenant continued in the son; the faithfulness of God to his sworn promise; the blessing passing from father to son. The verse displays the renewal of the covenant-promise to Isaac.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "I will be with thee, and will bless thee," God's presence and blessing (28:15, Gill, Clarke); "unto thee, and unto thy seed, I will give all these countries," the land confirmed (13:15, Calvin, Gill); "I will perform the oath which I sware unto Abraham," the covenant continued (22:16-18, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessed Isaac, with the seed and the nations (v.4).
4:
"And I will make thy seed to multiply as the stars of heaven, and will give unto thy seed all these countries; and in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed;" The witnesses note the promise of the seed and the blessing of the nations renewed to Isaac. On "I will make thy seed to multiply as the stars of heaven," the grammatical voices note the seed. Gill and Clarke note that "I will make thy seed to multiply as the stars of heaven, and will give unto thy seed all these countries" — God renewed to Isaac the promise of a numberless seed (as the stars, 15:5; 22:17) and the gift of the land; the covenant-promises (the seed and the land) confirmed to the son; the blessing on Isaac's posterity.

On "in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed," the reformed and witnesses note the gospel. Gill notes the great gospel promise — "in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed" (12:3; 22:18); the blessing of all nations in Isaac's seed; Paul declares the seed to be Christ (Galatians 3:16), in whom all nations are blessed; the covenant of grace, the gospel promise, confirmed to Isaac.

On the promise, the grammatical-historical note the continuity: the seed, the land, and the blessing of the nations — the threefold covenant-promise (made to Abraham, 12:2-3; 22:17-18) renewed entire to Isaac; the covenant carried in the son of promise; the gospel (the seed, Christ) confirmed; the line of the Messiah continued in Isaac. The verse displays the promise renewed to Isaac.

The verse displays the blessed Isaac, the ground being Abraham's obedience (v.5).
5:
"Because that Abraham obeyed my voice, and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws." The witnesses note Abraham's obedience as the ground of the blessing — the crux of obedience and covenant. On "Because that Abraham obeyed my voice," the grammatical voices note the ground. Gill and Clarke note that "Because that Abraham obeyed my voice" — God gives Abraham's obedience as the reason for renewing the covenant to Isaac; Abraham obeyed God's voice (in all things — the call, 12:1; the offering of Isaac, 22:18, "because thou hast obeyed my voice"); the obedience of faith the occasion of the blessing on the son.

On "kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws," interpreters expound the legal terms. The reformed Calvin and the grammatical-historical note the difficulty: the heaping of legal terms — "charge, commandments, statutes, laws" — describes Abraham's full, comprehensive obedience; the question how Abraham kept "commandments, statutes, laws" before the written law (Sinai) was given: (a) the terms are used generally/anachronistically, describing the patriarch's wholehearted obedience to whatever God commanded him (the obedience of faith); (b) Abraham kept the law written on the heart (the moral law, Romans 2:14-15), and the precepts and rites God gave him (circumcision, sacrifice); (c) some (the Jewish reading) take it of the whole law, as if Abraham kept it by anticipation. Calvin notes the obedience is commended, yet not as the meritorious cause — the covenant rests on grace (15:6, Abraham believed God), and his obedience is the fruit and evidence of faith, the way in which the blessing is continued, not its purchase.

On "obeyed my voice," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the example: "do thou do so too" — Abraham's obedience is set before Isaac (and all) as an example; the blessing flows in the channel of obedience; the obedient walk of faith the way of the covenant; yet the promise itself of free grace (the obedience the condition/fruit, not the merit). The verse displays Abraham's obedience as the ground of the blessing.

The witnesses agree the substance: God renewed the covenant to Isaac because Abraham obeyed his voice and kept his charge, commandments, statutes, and laws (Gill, Calvin, Clarke, Wesley). The phrase bears a crux: how Abraham kept "commandments, statutes, laws" before the written law — the terms used generally of his full obedience of faith, or of the law written on the heart and the precepts God gave him (Romans 2:14-15); and the relation of the obedience to the covenant — not the meritorious cause (the covenant of grace, 15:6), but the fruit and evidence of faith, the channel of the blessing. The verse displays the obedient Abraham, Isaac dwelling in Gerar (v.6).
6:

Gill and Henry note that "Isaac dwelt in Gerar" — Isaac obeyed God's command (v.2, dwell in the land), settling in Gerar (the Philistine country); the obedience of faith, staying in the land of promise rather than going to Egypt; the patriarch dwelling where God directed; the setting for the incident with Abimelech (vv.7-11).

On "Isaac dwelt in Gerar," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the staying. Henry and Gill note that Isaac dwelt in Gerar — in obedience to God's word (v.2); the son of promise kept in the land; the trial of faith (to dwell in the famine-struck land, trusting God); the obedience that brought God's blessing (v.12).

On the dwelling, the grammatical-historical note the providence: Isaac's dwelling in Gerar at God's command sets the scene for the chapter — the deception (vv.7-11), the prosperity (vv.12-14), the strife over wells (vv.15-22); the patriarch's sojourn under God's guidance; the obedience of faith carried out. The verse displays Isaac dwelling in Gerar in obedience.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 7:
"And the men of the place asked him of his wife; and he said, She is my sister: for he feared to say, She is my wife; lest, said he, the men of the place should kill me for Rebekah; because she was fair to look upon." The witnesses note Isaac's deception about his wife — the crux of the repeated fault. On "the men of the place asked him of his wife; and he said, She is my sister," the grammatical voices note the deception. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the men of the place asked him of his wife; and he said, She is my sister" — Isaac, asked about Rebekah, said she was his sister; the same deception his father Abraham had twice practiced (with Sarah, 12:13; 20:2); the strange repetition — Isaac falling into the very fault of his father; the dissembling of the patriarch.

On "for he feared to say, She is my wife," interpreters note the fault. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note that Isaac "feared to say, She is my wife; lest... the men of the place should kill me for Rebekah; because she was fair to look upon" — the same fear of man (Proverbs 29:25) that had driven Abraham; Isaac feared he would be killed for his fair wife; the weakness of faith, looking to the danger rather than trusting God's protection (who had just promised, v.3, I will be with thee); the deception born of fear; the saying "she is my sister" a lie (Rebekah was not his sister, but his wife and cousin — unlike Sarah, Abraham's half-sister, 20:12, so Isaac's word had no half-truth to lean on, the deception more plainly a falsehood, though some note the loose use of "sister" for kinswoman).

On the deception, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the example of inherited fault: Isaac's repeating his father's sin shows the strange persistence of the same fault in the family (the inherited weakness, the children stumbling where the fathers stumbled); the warning that even the godly fall into the faults of their parents; the deception that endangered Rebekah and the promise, yet was overruled by God (vv.8-11). The verse displays Isaac's deception about his wife.

The witnesses agree the substance: Isaac, fearing for his life, said Rebekah was his sister, repeating his father's fault (Keil, Clarke, Calvin, Gill). The deception bears a crux: the same fault of unbelief and the fear of man as Abraham's (12:13; 20:2; Proverbs 29:25), the strange repetition of the father's sin in the son; the saying a plain falsehood (Rebekah his wife and cousin, not his sister, with no half-truth as Abraham had, 20:12, though some note the loose use of "sister" for kinswoman); the weakness of faith, despite God's just-given promise (v.3). The verse displays the repeated-deception crux. The verse marks the saying: she is my sister. The verse displays the debate: the same fault as Abraham; a plain lie or a loose kinship-term. The verse displays the dissembling Isaac, discovered by Abimelech (v.8).
8:
"And it came to pass, when he had been there a long time, that Abimelech king of the Philistines looked out at a window, and saw, and, behold, Isaac was sporting with Rebekah his wife." The witnesses note Abimelech discovering Isaac and Rebekah were married. On "when he had been there a long time," the grammatical voices note the time. Ellicott and Gill note that "when he had been there a long time, that Abimelech... looked out at a window, and saw" — after Isaac had dwelt long in Gerar (the deception, v.7, maintained a while), Abimelech happened to look out a window and saw; the providence that exposed the deception in time; the long sojourn before the discovery.

On "behold, Isaac was sporting with Rebekah his wife," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the discovery. Wesley and Gill note that Abimelech saw Isaac "sporting with Rebekah his wife" — Isaac showing toward Rebekah such affection (caressing, conjugal endearment) as revealed she was his wife, not his sister; the deception exposed by Isaac's own conduct; the providence uncovering the truth; the husband's familiarity betraying the marriage.

On the discovery, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the exposure: Abimelech's seeing the conjugal "sporting" revealed the truth — that Rebekah was Isaac's wife; the deception (v.7) discovered; the providence of God exposing the fault before harm was done (as with Sarah, ch. 20); the patriarch's dissembling brought to light. The verse displays Abimelech discovering they were married.

9:
"And Abimelech called Isaac, and said, Behold, of a surety she is thy wife: and how saidst thou, She is my sister? And Isaac said unto him, Because I said, Lest I die for her." The witnesses note Abimelech rebuking Isaac for the deception. The grammatical voices note the rebuke. Gill notes that "Abimelech called Isaac, and said, Behold, of a surety she is thy wife: and how saidst thou, She is my sister?" — Abimelech, having discovered the truth (v.8), reproved Isaac: it was certain Rebekah was his wife; why had he said she was his sister? the king's just reproof of the deception; the patriarch reproved by the heathen king (as Abraham, 12:18; 20:9).

On "how saidst thou, She is my sister?" the witnesses note the reproof. Gill notes the king's reproof — why the deception? the just complaint against the falsehood; Isaac humbled, reproved for his fault by Abimelech.

On "Because I said, Lest I die for her," the grammatical-historical note the excuse: Isaac's answer, "Because I said, Lest I die for her" — the patriarch confessed his fear (that he would be killed for Rebekah, v.7); the fear of man (Proverbs 29:25) that drove the deception; the weak excuse of the godly man, owning the fault born of fear; the same plea as Abraham's (20:11). The verse displays Abimelech rebuking Isaac.

10:

Gill and Clarke note that "Abimelech said, What is this thou hast done unto us?" — the king reproached Isaac for the deception that had endangered him and his people; the just complaint (as Abraham was reproved, 12:18; 20:9); the patriarch's fault exposed.

11:

On "shall surely be put to death," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the penalty: "shall surely be put to death" — the severe penalty securing the protection; the king's earnest care; the patriarch and his wife safe under the decree; the providence overruling the deception for their safety; the covenant-couple preserved. The verse displays Abimelech's decree protecting Isaac and Rebekah.

12:
